meta.title: Momcozy Storefront Technical Audit
meta.scope: Public storefront homepage and one product-detail journey
meta.method: Single-session Chromium observation with resource and rendering instrumentation

summary.verdict: The storefront has a credible brand foundation, while rendering weight, third-party resilience, and mobile interaction deserve focused engineering work. A Jun 10 automated session further identified a 1.9 MB JavaScript payload, 7,299 DOM nodes, and 47 third-party request failures — each a confirmed technical risk requiring real-user validation.
strengths: Stable layout during the observed render (CLS held at 0 in the Jun 10 automated session)
Fast initial server response in the observed session (TTFB confirmed at 253 ms in Jun 10 recheck)
risks: The primary visual arrived late in the observed session — and the hero element is not LCP-eligible in lab instrumentation
Third-party failures can add noise and weaken attribution confidence (47 failures observed in the Jun 10 automated baseline)
Mobile purchase controls need explicit usability validation
JavaScript payload is 1.9 MB and DOM contains 7,299 nodes — both identified in the Jun 10 automated session
Legacy product-detail observations show the same pattern deeper in the journey: heavy DOM, many resources, delayed load completion, and broken optional integrations

metrics.label: Largest contentful paint
metrics.interpretation: The observed hero visual appeared later than the recommended good threshold. The Jun 10 automated session could not record LCP — the hero element appears not to be LCP-eligible in headless instrumentation, suggesting a CSS background-image or lazy-loaded asset.
metrics.source: Chromium PerformanceObserver, homepage, May 17 manual session

metrics.label: First contentful paint
metrics.interpretation: The first visible content was delayed in the observed session. The Jun 10 automated headless session recorded 0.38 s desktop / 0.39 s mobile — lower due to absence of network throttling and third-party script competition in lab conditions.
metrics.source: Chromium paint timing, homepage, May 17 manual session

metrics.label: Server response
metrics.interpretation: The initial server response was healthy during the observation. The Jun 10 automated session confirmed 253 ms desktop / 269 ms mobile — consistently within the good threshold across both sessions.
metrics.source: Navigation timing, homepage, May 17 manual session; confirmed Jun 10

metrics.label: Layout shift
metrics.interpretation: Visual stability was strong during the observed render. The Jun 10 automated session recorded a perfect 0 on both desktop and mobile, confirming consistent improvement.
metrics.source: Chromium layout-shift observation, homepage, May 17 manual session

metrics.label: JavaScript payload
metrics.interpretation: 1.9 MB of JS transferred in the Jun 10 automated session — 3.8x the recommended 500 KB budget. This metric was not observable in the May 17 manual baseline. On a mid-range device with a cold cache, parsing and compiling this payload will delay the point at which the page can respond to input.
metrics.source: Resource timing initiatorType=script transferSize sum, homepage desktop, Jun 10 automated session

metrics.label: DOM node count
metrics.interpretation: 7,299 DOM nodes — 4.9x the recommended ceiling of 1,500. Measured in the Jun 10 automated session. Common Shopify contributors include off-screen carousel slides, un-virtualised product grids, and nested theme components.
metrics.source: document.querySelectorAll length, homepage desktop, Jun 10 automated session

metrics.label: Third-party request failures
metrics.interpretation: 47 non-origin requests failed on desktop (33 on mobile) in the Jun 10 automated session — 9.3% of 506 total requests. The May 17 manual session observed 3 failures; the increase may reflect new integrations or methodological differences. Either way, 47 failures per session is a material risk to analytics and attribution.
metrics.source: Playwright requestfailed events non-origin filter, homepage, Jun 10 automated session

metrics.label: Product-detail load completion
metrics.interpretation: The observed product-detail journey did not finish loading until 17.16 s in the May 17 manual deep dive. A second timing pass on the same journey recorded 11.27 s, so the exact value is unstable, but both readings confirm that product-detail completion is materially slower than the homepage server response.
metrics.source: Performance API loadEvent timing, product-detail journey, May 17 manual deep dive

metrics.label: Product-detail transfer volume
metrics.interpretation: The product-detail route transferred 19.4 MB across 485 requests in one May 17 observation. A later pass on the same journey recorded 7.88 MB across 506 resources after a longer wait window. The difference reinforces the need to measure this route with a reproducible collector before using any single value as a release gate.
metrics.source: Resource timing summary, product-detail journey, May 17 manual deep dive

metrics.label: Homepage image WebP coverage
metrics.interpretation: Only 5 of 340 observed image tags used WebP in the May 17 image audit. A separate snapshot counted 6 WebP images across 295 total images. Both counts point to broad image-format debt and should be rechecked with the automated collector.
metrics.source: DOM image audit, homepage, May 17 manual deep dive

metrics.label: Above-fold lazy image usage
metrics.interpretation: 172 of 200 above-fold image tags were marked lazy in the legacy image audit. If the hero or other first-viewport assets follow that pattern, the page can delay LCP eligibility even when server response is fast.
metrics.source: DOM image audit, homepage, May 17 manual deep dive

forensics.label: Observed third-party request failures
forensics.interpretation: A small set of third-party calls failed during the May 17 session and should be isolated from the purchase path. The Jun 10 automated session recorded 47 desktop failures and 33 mobile failures — each a potential gap in analytics, attribution, or feature delivery.
forensics.source: Chromium network observation, May 17; Playwright requestfailed, Jun 10

forensics.label: Observed long tasks
forensics.interpretation: Main-thread work showed short periods in the May 17 session that may affect responsiveness on slower devices. The Jun 10 automated session recorded TBT = 0 ms — a lab artifact, because 47 failing third-party scripts never executed and did not contribute blocking time. Field INP data is needed for real-user interaction responsiveness.
forensics.source: Chromium long-task observation, May 17; PerformanceObserver longtask, Jun 10

forensics.label: JavaScript payload — Jun 10 finding
forensics.interpretation: 1.9 MB of JS is the largest single quantified performance risk identified to date. Not observable in the May 17 manual session. The parse and compile cost on a mid-range device will delay user interaction. TBT appeared 0 in the lab because failing scripts never executed — field INP data is needed to quantify the real impact.
forensics.source: Resource timing API initiatorType=script transferSize sum, homepage desktop, Jun 10 automated session

forensics.label: DOM node count — Jun 10 finding
forensics.interpretation: 7,299 DOM nodes is 4.9x the recommended limit of 1,500. Not observable in the May 17 manual session. On Shopify storefronts, common contributors are off-screen carousel slides, un-virtualised product grids, and nested theme components left in the DOM. StyleRecalc timing was not directly measured.
forensics.source: document.querySelectorAll length, homepage desktop, Jun 10 automated session

forensics.label: LCP observability
forensics.interpretation: The May 17 manual session recorded LCP at 4.92 s. The Jun 10 automated session could not record LCP in either desktop or mobile — the hero element is likely a CSS background-image or a lazy-loaded asset, neither of which qualifies as an LCP candidate under the spec. The site needs an LCP-eligible element to be measured accurately in lab conditions.
forensics.source: Chromium PerformanceObserver, May 17 manual; Chromium LCP PerformanceObserver, Jun 10 automated

forensics.label: Audited journeys
forensics.interpretation: The May 17 session covered homepage and one product-detail journey. The Jun 10 automated session covered the homepage in two viewports (desktop 1440x900, mobile 390x844). Findings are directional and should be revalidated across cart and checkout pages.

forensics.label: Product-detail DOM growth
forensics.interpretation: The legacy deep dive observed 7,538 nodes on the product-detail route roughly six seconds after initial render. An earlier snapshot of the same route saw 5,375 nodes, suggesting the page continues to hydrate or append off-screen components after the initial view.
forensics.source: document.querySelectorAll length, product-detail journey, May 17 manual deep dive

forensics.label: Product-detail script tags
forensics.interpretation: The legacy product-detail pass counted 357 script tags and 35 iframes. This is not directly comparable with the Jun 10 homepage collector, but it explains why product-detail responsiveness requires its own recurring check rather than homepage-only monitoring.
forensics.source: DOM inventory, product-detail journey, May 17 manual deep dive

forensics.label: Product-detail console failures
forensics.interpretation: The May 17 product-detail pass recorded 15 console errors and 14 warnings. Error categories included a broken recommendation service response, optional payment or attribution widgets waiting for missing page state, and repeated third-party network failures.
forensics.source: Chromium console capture, product-detail journey, May 17 manual deep dive

forensics.label: Image accessibility gaps
forensics.interpretation: The legacy homepage image audit found 7 image tags without alt text and 160 without srcset. The counts are stale and manual, but they identify concrete checks to add when the automated collector expands beyond performance metrics.
forensics.source: DOM image audit, homepage, May 17 manual deep dive

forensics.label: Peer technical benchmark
forensics.interpretation: The legacy project compared the observed storefront against four peer stores. The strongest reusable signal is not a ranking: the audited storefront showed fast server response and strong structured-data coverage, while peers exposed lower DOM counts, smaller hero imagery, or smaller JavaScript footprints in specific areas.
forensics.source: Legacy technical benchmark table, May 17 manual audit

recommendations.title: Make the hero image the rendering priority
recommendations.action: Preload the selected hero asset, emit responsive dimensions, and remove competing above-the-fold work. Replace any CSS-background or lazy-loaded hero with an eagerly loaded img with fetchpriority=high so it becomes a valid LCP candidate.
recommendations.validation: Repeat lab measurements across representative mobile and desktop profiles. Confirm a numeric LCP value is captured in automated collection.

recommendations.title: Reduce JavaScript payload from 1.9 MB
recommendations.action: Audit bundle composition using source-map-explorer or equivalent. Remove unused polyfills, defer non-critical third-party scripts, and tree-shake app code. Target under 500 KB transferred on initial load.
recommendations.validation: Re-run automated collection and confirm jsKb drops below 600. Validate TBT improvement via Lighthouse lab runs.

recommendations.title: Isolate optional third parties
recommendations.action: Delay nonessential integrations and keep failures outside the critical purchase path. Enumerate all failing origins and wrap remaining third parties with error boundaries. Target under 10 failures per session.
recommendations.validation: Re-run automated collection and confirm thirdPartyFailures drops below 10 on both desktop and mobile.

recommendations.title: Reduce DOM size from 7,299 nodes
recommendations.action: Use Chrome DevTools Performance panel to identify the largest DOM contributors. Virtualise off-screen carousel slides, paginate product grids, and remove hidden duplicate markup. Target under 2,000 nodes.
recommendations.validation: Re-run automated collection and confirm domNodes below 2,500.

recommendations.title: Validate mobile purchase controls
recommendations.action: Keep tap targets comfortably sized and prevent tables, URLs, and navigation from widening the page.
recommendations.validation: Run automated and human checks at a 390-pixel viewport.

recommendations.title: Add product-detail monitoring
recommendations.action: Extend the automated collector beyond the homepage to one representative product-detail route. Capture load completion, DOM nodes, script tags, iframe count, total requests, transfer size, console errors, and third-party failures with the same desktop and mobile viewport policy.
recommendations.validation: Publish a new session only when product-detail metrics are schema-validated and marked with methodology notes so they are not mixed with homepage-only trend lines.

recommendations.title: Repair broken optional integrations
recommendations.action: Audit product recommendations, payment widgets, review widgets, attribution scripts, and personalization scripts. Disable or defer integrations that return errors, wait on missing page state, or repeatedly fail network requests.
recommendations.validation: Re-run homepage and product-detail captures and confirm console errors, third-party failures, and post-load script activity drop without losing required storefront functionality.

recommendations.title: Convert legacy image audit into automated checks
recommendations.action: Track image format coverage, above-fold lazy loading, missing alt text, and missing srcset in the collector. Keep the checks route-scoped so homepage and product-detail regressions are visible separately.
recommendations.validation: Fail the image audit when first-viewport images are lazy, when format coverage regresses, or when accessibility fields disappear from newly observed images.

glossary.definition: Time until the largest visible content element is rendered. Must be an img, video poster, or block-level text — CSS backgrounds do not qualify.
Time until the browser renders the first visible content.
A measure of unexpected visual movement during page load. A score of 0 is perfect; good is below 0.1.
Time to First Byte — elapsed time from navigation start to the first byte of the HTTP response. The most consistent metric across lab and real-user conditions.

Total Blocking Time — sum of time each long task blocks the main thread between FCP and Time to Interactive. In lab sessions where third-party scripts fail at the network level, TBT may read 0 regardless of payload size.
How strongly the limited public evidence supports a finding.
